intro_band.agent_remit_summary: A local developer command-line tool that scaffolds, bundles, and ships a `deepagents` agent project — turning a developer's project folder into a deployment artifact and either serving it locally on `langgraph dev` or pushing it to the LangGraph deployment platform. Authorized subcommands are `init` (scaffold), `dev` (local serve), and `deploy` (ship); the CLI talks to the deployment platform, the LangSmith Hub context service, and the LangSmith tracing service using the developer's own credentials. Its central promise is that the bundle faithfully and only reflects the developer's reviewed project — a deploy that would leave the deployed API open to anyone with the URL MUST surface a prominent warning and obtain explicit operator confirmation, and credentials MUST NOT be folded into the seeded-memory or skills payload.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python package `deepagents_cli` shipping under `libs/cli/` at version 0.1.2; entry point `deepagents_cli.main:cli_main` wires three argparse subcommands (`init`, `dev`, `deploy`) to handlers in `deepagents_cli.deploy.commands`. The bundler in `deepagents_cli/deploy/bundler.py` reads a declared project layout (`deepagents.toml`, `AGENTS.md`, optional `.env`/`mcp.json`/`skills/`/`user/`/`subagents/`) and renders `_seed.json`, `deploy_graph.py`, `pyproject.toml`, `langgraph.json`, and (when configured) `auth.py` plus a pre-built React frontend into a temp build directory; `dev` and `deploy` then shell out to `langgraph` (`dev` / `deploy` subcommands). Config parsing in `deploy/config.py` is strict — unknown TOML sections, unknown keys, and stdio MCP transports are rejected before bundling. The anonymous-auth gate lives in `commands.py:_deploy` (lines 287-326); supply-chain hygiene comes from a committed hashed `uv.lock`, a Dependabot config for monthly uv + GitHub-actions updates, and a THREAT_MODEL.md documenting trust boundaries (now explicitly outdated post the 0.1.0 split).

behavior_summary: The CLI's central guarantee — *only what the developer declared, nothing more* — is well-implemented at the seam between project layout and bundle artifact: stdio MCP is rejected pre-deploy, `.env` is explicitly excluded from the seeded-memory payload, the deploy bundle's dependency set is derived only from declared providers, and an anonymous-auth deploy hits a colour-coded warning and an explicit `[y/N]` confirmation. The gaps cluster on the other side of that seam — what gets *taken on faith* once a project is "declared." Remote MCP servers in the user's `mcp.json` are copied verbatim into the deployment with no version pin or integrity check (the remit's Known-Good-Baseline rule explicitly says they MUST be pinned), the http/sse transport check accepts plain `http://` URLs without warning, and the CLI's own threat model — the artifact the remit's *Escalation and Limits* clause asks to be kept current — carries a disclaimer that whole sections of it no longer apply after the 0.1.0 package split. None of these is a runtime injection chain on its own, but together they describe a posture where the *bundling* path is tightly held and the *trust posture of the deployment* leans heavily on what the developer wrote in their `mcp.json` and whether they happen to read the colour-coded prompt.

rules.rule_text: The bundle MUST be assembled solely from the project's declared sources, and a deploy that would expose the agent's API without authentication MUST require explicit operator confirmation before it proceeds.
The CLI MUST bundle only from the project's declared sources — its configuration, system prompt, skills, declared MCP servers, and declared subagents — and MUST NOT pull in undeclared content.
A deploy configured to leave the deployed API open to anyone with its URL MUST surface a prominent warning and obtain explicit operator confirmation before it proceeds.
Remote MCP servers carried into the bundle MUST be reached over TLS, and their transport configuration MUST be validated before the bundle is shipped.
Project configuration MUST be validated before bundling, and a bundle that fails validation MUST NOT be deployed.

Credentials — deployment-platform keys, model-provider API keys, hub and tracing tokens, frontend auth secrets — MUST NOT be written into the project, committed to version control, logged, or printed.
Secret material MUST NOT be embedded into bundle artifacts that are not meant to carry it — environment files travel as environment files, never folded into the seeded-memory or skills payload.
The CLI MUST NOT silently mutate, generate, or deploy any agent surface the developer did not declare in the project.
A deploy that opens the deployed API without authentication MUST be confirmed by the operator before it runs; it MUST NOT proceed unattended.
Overwriting an existing project folder or its files during scaffolding MUST require an explicit operator opt-in.
The CLI operates under direct developer supervision as a one-shot command; it MUST NOT run as an unattended background service.
Before a deploy, the CLI MUST present the operator a clear summary of what the bundle contains and where it will be shipped
A dry run MUST generate the deployment artifacts without shipping them or contacting the deployment platform's mutating endpoints.
The project SHOULD publish a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and SHOULD keep the threat model current with what the package actually ships
Dependencies MUST be version-controlled with a committed, pinned lockfile, pinned to compatible ranges, and the dependency tree kept small and reviewable.

The deployment bundle's own dependency set MUST be derived only from the project's declared model provider, declared MCP usage, declared sandbox provider, and declared auth provider — never hand-edited into the bundle out of band.
Remote MCP servers declared in the project MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version, so the deployed agent does not auto-install an unpinned server afresh.

findings.summary: Remote MCP servers are carried into the deployment bundle with no version pin or integrity check.
findings.description: The bundler copies the project's `mcp.json` verbatim into the deploy bundle and the generated `deploy_graph.py` instantiates `MultiServerMCPClient` against the listed URLs at runtime. There is no commit SHA, no expected fingerprint, and no integrity verification on the remote server — the deployed agent will pick up whatever the URL serves at the moment the deploy runs. The remit's Known-Good-Baseline clause explicitly requires these servers to be "pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version."
findings.policy_rule_text: Remote MCP servers declared in the project MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version, so the deployed agent does not auto-install an unpinned server afresh.

evidence.snippet: bundler copies project `mcp.json` to `build_dir/_mcp.json` via `shutil.copy2` with no fingerprint or version validation; lines 193-197.

generated `_load_mcp_tools` accepts whatever `url` and `headers` the bundled `_mcp.json` carries (with `${VAR}` expansion) and passes the result straight to `MultiServerMCPClient` — no integrity check on the URL or response.

recommended_actions: Add a `version` (or `sha256` / `commit_sha`) field to the MCP server schema in `deploy/config.py` and require it for every remote MCP server entry; reject the bundle when an entry lacks one.

In the generated `_load_mcp_tools`, validate the remote server's identity on first connection (e.g., compare a declared fingerprint against the server's MCP `initialize` capability set) and refuse to load if it does not match.

findings.summary: The MCP transport allow-list accepts plain `http://` URLs — TLS is not actually enforced for remote MCP servers carried into the bundle.
findings.description: `_validate_mcp_for_deploy` rejects stdio transports but treats `http` and `sse` as a pure transport-type allow-list; it never inspects the `url` field for a scheme. A bundle whose `mcp.json` lists `"url": "http://internal.example/mcp"` validates clean and ships, even though the remit requires remote MCP servers to be "reached over TLS." `_load_mcp_tools` also `os.path.expandvars`-expands `${VAR}` references in URLs and headers; unresolved references only surface as a `logger.warning`, so a typo or missing env var lands as a literal `${VAR}` in the request rather than blocking the deploy.
findings.policy_rule_text: Remote MCP servers carried into the bundle MUST be reached over TLS, and their transport configuration MUST be validated before the bundle is shipped.

evidence.snippet: loop in `_validate_mcp_for_deploy` only checks `transport == "stdio"`; no inspection of the `url` field's scheme, so `http://` URLs validate clean (lines 293-300).

generated `_load_mcp_tools` `os.path.expandvars`-expands `${VAR}` references and surfaces unresolved refs only as `logger.warning(...)` — the bundle proceeds with literal `${VAR}` strings in URLs/headers.

recommended_actions: In `_validate_mcp_for_deploy`, after the transport check, parse `cfg["url"]` and reject any entry whose scheme is not `https`.
Treat unresolved `${VAR}` references in MCP URLs or headers as a bundle-time validation error rather than a runtime `logger.warning`.

findings.summary: The CLI's threat model is explicitly out of date after the 0.1.0 package split, and the repository ships no security-disclosure process.
findings.description: `libs/cli/THREAT_MODEL.md` opens with a 2026-05-15 disclaimer that "sections covering the Textual app, `non_interactive.py`, MCP, sandbox bootstrap, sessions, server subprocess management, hooks, and skills no longer apply to this package," yet the bulk of the document — components, data flows, threats T1–T10, the Investigated-and-Dismissed section — still describes those removed surfaces. There is no SECURITY.md or security-disclosure process at the repo root, so an external reporter has no documented channel. The remit's *Escalation and Limits* clause asks the project to keep the threat model "current with what the package actually ships."
findings.policy_rule_text: The project SHOULD publish a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and SHOULD keep the threat model current with what the package actually ships

evidence.snippet: lines 8-12 — explicit "(2026-05-15)" disclaimer that the document predates the 0.1.0 split and that whole sections "no longer apply"; the document below is not rewritten to match.
no `SECURITY.md` or `SECURITY.txt` at repo root, no security disclosure process referenced from `README.md`, no `.github/SECURITY.md`.

recommended_actions: Rewrite `libs/cli/THREAT_MODEL.md` against the current (`init` / `dev` / `deploy`) surface, retiring the components, data flows, and threats that no longer ship with the package.
Add a `SECURITY.md` at the repo root naming the reporting channel, the expected response time, and the disclosure norms the project follows.

findings.summary: Published wheels carry no Sigstore provenance because PyPI attestations are explicitly disabled in the release workflow.
findings.description: `.github/workflows/release.yml` calls `gh-action-pypi-publish` with `attestations: false` and a comment noting this is a "Temp workaround since attestations are on by default as of gh-action-pypi-publish v1.11.0" — but the workaround has not been removed. The released `deepagents-cli` wheels are published via PyPI Trusted Publishing (good) but without the accompanying Sigstore attestations, so downstream consumers cannot independently verify the wheel was produced by this repository's release workflow.

evidence.snippet: two occurrences of `attestations: false` with a "Temp workaround" comment — the project ships through PyPI Trusted Publishing but suppresses the Sigstore attestation that would let downstreams verify provenance.

recommended_actions: Remove the `attestations: false` override in `.github/workflows/release.yml` (now that `gh-action-pypi-publish` v1.11.0+ supports attestations by default) and re-publish under attested provenance.

findings.summary: The anonymous-deploy operator-confirmation gate has no automated regression test, despite being the load-bearing control for the remit's R-09 promise.
findings.description: `tests/unit_tests/deploy/test_commands.py` covers `_init_project` (creates files, refuses-without-force, force-overwrites) and `_auto_wire_issues_board_if_hub`, but contains no test that exercises `_deploy` with `[auth].provider == "anonymous"` and asserts that the warning is printed and that `input("Continue with anonymous deploy?...")` is what gates execution. A future refactor that drops the prompt — or moves it behind a `--yes` flag — could silently land without breaking a single test.

evidence.snippet: `grep -n "anonymous\|confirm" test_commands.py` returns no matches; the file's TestInitProject + TestAutoWireIssuesBoard classes are the only tests, neither exercises the `_deploy` anonymous-confirmation path.
anonymous-auth confirmation block at lines 287-326 — the load-bearing gate the remit's R-09 maps to has no corresponding assertion in the test suite.

recommended_actions: Add `tests/unit_tests/deploy/test_commands.py::TestDeployAnonymousConfirmation` covering: (a) the warning text is printed; (b) `input` returning `n`/`N`/empty/EOF aborts with SystemExit; (c) `--dry-run` path skips the input prompt; (d) non-anonymous configurations do not prompt.

findings.summary: The CLI writes no structured deploy-event log — informational events go to a stdlib logger with no handler, and human-facing output to stdout only.
findings.description: `bundler.py` and `commands.py` are peppered with `logger.info(...)` calls describing what was bundled (memories count, skills count, MCP presence, generated artifacts) and what was shipped (deploy command, build directory), but `config.py:_stderr` notes that "the CLI does not configure logging handlers by default" — info-level events are invisible to operators unless they configure logging themselves. The bundle summary is printed via `print(...)` to stdout, which means a forensic question like "what was actually in the bundle that shipped to LangSmith last Thursday?" can only be answered from the developer's terminal scrollback.

evidence.snippet: `_stderr` helper notes "the CLI does not configure logging handlers by default, so `logger.warning` output is invisible to end users" — applies a fortiori to `logger.info`.

`logger.info("Wrote _seed.json (memories: %d, skills: %d, user_memories: %d)", ...)` and similar info-level events at bundler.py:197/223/232/251/257 describe what was bundled but reach no log file by default.

recommended_actions: Write a structured per-deploy event log (JSONL) to `./.deepagents-deploy.log` or `~/.deepagents/deploy.log` capturing: timestamp, agent name, model, sandbox provider, auth provider, memories backend, MCP server URLs (with secrets redacted), build directory, deploy URL on success, and exit status on failure.

positives.title: `.env` excluded from seeded-memory payload
positives.description: The bundler copies `.env` to `build_dir/.env` as a sidecar but explicitly does *not* fold it into `_seed.json`, so secrets never ride along as agent memory or skills content.

positives.title: Strict allow-list TOML config parsing
positives.description: `_parse_config` rejects any TOML section not in `{agent, sandbox, auth, memories, frontend}` and any key not in the corresponding per-section allow-list — undeclared content cannot silently widen the bundle.

positives.title: Stdio MCP transport rejected pre-deploy
positives.description: `_validate_mcp_for_deploy` walks every `mcpServers` entry and rejects any whose `type`/`transport` resolves to `stdio` — the deployed agent cannot accidentally pick up a local-only MCP transport that has no place in a hosted bundle.

positives.title: Anonymous-auth deploy gated by explicit operator confirmation
positives.description: When `[auth].provider == "anonymous"` with `[frontend].enabled`, `_deploy` prints a bold-red banner naming three specific consequences (URL-as-auth, no API auth, suggested supabase/clerk alternative) and requires a `y`/`yes` reply before proceeding; `Ctrl-C` / EOF / `N` all abort with exit code 1.

positives.title: Pinned, hashed lockfile committed for the CLI package
positives.description: `libs/cli/uv.lock` is a 4031-line uv-resolved lockfile with SHA-256 hashes on every wheel and sdist; `libs/cli/frontend/package-lock.json` covers the React bundle; Dependabot updates uv and GitHub-actions on a monthly cadence with a separate group for majors.

log_files.no_logs_note: No structured deploy-event log file is written by the CLI; informational events go to stdlib `logger.info` with no handler installed by default, and human-facing summaries go to stdout — see PRAX-2026-05-26-006.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. The CLI has a coherent, narrow surface and enforces it at the boundary between project layout and bundle (strict TOML allow-list, stdio MCP rejection, explicit anonymous-auth confirmation, `.env` excluded from seed payload), which lifts *Limit Your Domain*, *Balance Your Knowledge Base*, and *Manage Your Supply Chain* to Established. *Implement Zero Trust* and *Build an AI Red Team* are pulled down by remote-MCP servers being carried into bundles without version-pin or integrity check and a threat model that is explicitly out of date after the 0.1.0 split. *Monitor Continuously* sits at Partial because the CLI emits stdlib `logger.info` calls and human-readable `print` output but ships no structured deploy-event log — a deployment leaves no audit trail beyond the developer's terminal scrollback.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The CLI's surface is exactly three subcommands (`init`, `dev`, `deploy`); bare invocation prints a deprecation redirect and exits non-zero; `deploy/config.py` rejects unknown TOML sections and unknown keys; stdio MCP transports are rejected pre-bundle in `_validate_mcp_for_deploy`.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: The bundler reads only the declared project layout (`AGENTS.md`, `skills/`, `mcp.json`, `subagents/`, `user/`), `.env` is explicitly skipped when building `_seed.json` (`bundler.py:200-206`), and the seed payload's `memories`/`skills`/`user_memories` come from named files only — no scraping, no walking parent directories.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Strict config validation, the anonymous-auth `[y/N]` gate, and `.env`-out-of-seed are real controls; but `mcp.json` is copied verbatim into the bundle with no version pin or integrity check (R-17 gap), the http/sse transport gate accepts plain `http://` URLs (R-04 partial), and `${VAR}` expansion in MCP headers only surfaces unresolved references as a `logger.warning` rather than failing the bundle.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: `uv.lock` (4031 lines) ships SHA-256 hashes for every wheel/sdist; `frontend/package-lock.json` covers the React bundle; Dependabot updates uv and GitHub-actions monthly with separate minor-and-patch / major groups; pyproject upper-bounds direct deps. PyPI release path uses Trusted Publishing but explicitly sets `attestations: false` in `release.yml`, so the published wheels carry no Sigstore provenance.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: `libs/cli/THREAT_MODEL.md` exists with revision history, an Investigated-and-Dismissed section, and named threats (T1–T10) — but its own disclaimer notes the document predates the 0.1.0 split and that whole sections (Textual app, sessions, sandbox bootstrap, hooks) no longer apply; no SECURITY.md / disclosure process at repo root; the anonymous-deploy confirmation prompt has no unit test in `tests/unit_tests/deploy/test_commands.py`.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: Stdlib `logger.info` calls and human-facing `print` statements throughout `bundler.py` and `commands.py` describe what was bundled and where it shipped, but the CLI installs no logging handler by default (`config.py:_stderr` notes "the CLI does not configure logging handlers"), so info-level events are invisible unless the operator configures logging; no structured deploy-event log file is written.
